next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The brace was off. Petra had cleared him to leave it off for short stretches, and the graft-driven repair had run six weeks too fast, leaving an itch deep in the bone where new tissue met old. The spot held a busy, needling pressure, his body hurrying through a timetable he hadn't agreed to.

Kai sat on the edge of his bed and put his left hand flat on the mattress.

He'd started three nights ago, after his hand went early for a glass of water and he caught the quick flutter in the rebuilt forearm. Since then he'd been trying to catch that flutter before the motion finished.

Index finger. He told himself to lift it, then waited. There was a gathering low in the synthetic muscle, too small for anyone else to see. He pressed the finger flat to the mattress and held. One beat. Two. The pressure stalled against him.

The finger lifted when he let it.

A thread of signal. Each night it got easier to feel, a faint touch gathering before the motion. He could find the place where the rebuilt muscle wanted to go ahead of him and meet it there.

His shoulder clicked as he reached for the wrist brace. The joint had started settling into place with engineered precision. The cuts on his cheek and brow were already down to thin pink lines. He was healing on graft time, being rebuilt around the damage.

He put the brace back on and went to dinner.

---

Yusuf confirms virus spreading through shared calibration protocols

prev_tail_excerpt:
...s through the network, it—" He took his hands off the terminal. "It's already everywhere."

The room went very quiet.

Kai looked past Yusuf at the names on the screen. Novak. Priya. People he'd been on the track with an hour ago. People whose bodies were running on the same infrastructure that had just cut him out and kept going.

"Can you fix it?"

"I don't know yet." Professional smoothness was gone now. "We need to—look, nobody trains. Nobody calibrates. Nobody plugs into this network again until I've mapped the scope. Every handshake is another chance for it to copy."

"Every athlete."

"Everyone who's been calibrated here. Which is everyone." He finally pushed his glasses back into place. His fingers weren't steady. "I need Petra and the facility director. We lock the place down now or somebody else hits a wall."

Kai closed his eyes. The darkness behind them was ordinary, chosen. He could feel his eyelids, the stitch pull above his left eye, the dried blood on his lashes, the table under his skull, the weight of his own legs beneath the sheet. He opened his eyes again.

Yusuf unclipped the cable from the port at Kai's neck. The click was small and familiar.

"Get some rest," he said.

He took the tablet and went out. The door shut behind him.

Kai looked down the length of the exam table. The sheet over his legs rose in two low ridges. Both feet pointed where they were supposed to point. Still.

He pressed the call button with his good hand.

When the speaker answered, he said, "Could someone stay in here a bit?"

He kept his eyes on his feet and waited for the door.

setups_to_plant.description: Margot's direct neural feed connection may be an additional vector for virus propagation between athletes

state_deltas_required: The virus is systemic; every enhanced athlete at the facility is potentially compromised
From isolated incident to facility-wide existential threat
